s.def: Used to connect a noun to an adjective that describes it.

Used to form the perfect tense of the passive voice, together with a past participle.

Used to form the continuous forms of various tenses, together with aan het or, in archaic style, with a present participle.
examples: De man was aan het lopen.

examples: Ik ben even naar de dokter.

zijn.e: From Middle Dutch sijn, from Old Dutch sīn. The infinitive zijn along with the words is and zij (present indicative and subjunctive) derive ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁es- (“to be”), which had no separate infinitive in Germanic. The modern infinitive was probably back-formed in late Ol...

zimbabwe.e: From Shona dzimba-dze-mabwe (“house of stones”), in reference to Great Zimbabwe.

zaïre.e: Borrowed from French Zaïre; ultimately from Kongo nzere, a truncation of nzadi o nzere (“river swallowing rivers”).

zebra.e: Borrowed from Portuguese zebra, from Old Galician-Portuguese enzebro, ezebra, azebra (“wild ass”), from earlier cebrario (882), ezebrario (897), from Vulgar Latin *eciferus, from Latin equiferus (“wild horse”). The second sense is a shortening of zebrapad.

zak.e: From Middle Dutch sac, from Old Dutch sac, from Proto-West Germanic *sakku, from Proto-Germanic *sakkuz, from Latin saccus.

zoom.e: From Middle Dutch sôom, from Old Dutch *sōm, from Proto-West Germanic *saum, from Proto-Germanic *saumaz (“that which is sewn”).

s.def: used to form the future tense of a verb, together with an infinitive; will, going to
examples: Ik zal zingen.
Het zal toch niet werken.

s.def: used to form the conditional mood of a verb, together with an infinitive; would
examples: Het werk zou nu af zijn geweest als jij je niet had verslapen.
Dat zou ik niet doen als ik jou was.

examples: Het zou vandaag regenen.

zullen.e: From Middle Dutch solen, suelen, sollen, sullen, from Old Dutch sulan, from Proto-West Germanic *skulan, from Proto-Germanic *skulaną. Compare German sollen, English shall, Danish skulle.

s.def: The twenty-sixth letter of the Dutch alphabet, written in the Latin script.
The twenty-sixth and last letter of the Dutch alphabet, written in the Latin script; preceded by Y.

examples: Ik zie jouw kat!

zien.e:
From Middle Dutch sien, from Old Dutch sian, from Frankish *sehan, from Proto-West Germanic *sehwan, from Proto-Germanic *sehwaną, from Proto-Indo-European *sekʷ- (“to see, notice”).
Originally a class 5 strong verb, but the Germanic *h was lost from the infinitive and present tense forms through...

examples: Hij werkte zes dagen per week en heeft nu een burnout.
Hollanders gaan vaak rond zessen avondeten.

zes.e: From Middle Dutch ses, from Old Dutch ses, from Proto-West Germanic *sehs, from Proto-Germanic *sehs, from Proto-Indo-European *swéḱs.

examples: Deze winkel is zeven dagen per week open.
Ik werd iets na zevenen wakker.

zeven.e: From Middle Dutch sēven, from Old Dutch sivun, sivon, from Proto-West Germanic *sebun, from Proto-Germanic *sebun, from earlier *sebunt, from Proto-Indo-European *septḿ̥, with -t added through influence of "nine" and "ten".

zestien.e: From Middle Dutch sestien, from Old Dutch *sestēn, *sestien, from Proto-Germanic *sehstehun. Compare West Frisian sechstjin, German sechzehn, English sixteen, Danish seksten.

zeventien.e: From Middle Dutch seventien, from Old Dutch *sivontēn, *sivontien, from Proto-Germanic *sebuntehun. Compare English seventeen, West Frisian santjin, German siebzehn, Danish sytten.

s.def: a zebra crossing, a crosswalk (place where pedestrians can cross a street)

zebrapad.e: Compound of zebra (“zebra”) + pad (“path”). The choice of the element zebra was influenced by English zebra crossing.

zoo.e: Probably a borrowing from French zoo. Equivalent to a shortening of zoölogische tuin.

zepto-.e: From Ancient Greek ἑπτά (heptá, “seven”), for the seventh order of 10⁻³. The final o conforms to the finals vowel of the SI series from micro- downwards; the letter z was added, as a start in a series of terms running backwards through the alphabet.

s.def: Zeus (Supreme ruler of all Greek gods, husband to Hera)

zeus.e: From Middle Dutch zeus, from Ancient Greek Ζεύς (Zeús).

zenit.e: Borrowed from Latin cenit, from Arabic سَمْت (samt).

a hamlet in Berg en Dal, Gelderland, Netherlands

zeeland.e: From Middle Dutch Sêelant. Equivalent to zee (“sea”) + land (“land”) or from zeeland (“coastal land”).

zona.e: Borrowed from Latin zōna, from Ancient Greek ζώνη (zṓnē).

zon.e: From Middle Dutch sonne, from Old Dutch sunna, from Proto-West Germanic *sunnā, from Proto-Germanic *sunnǭ, from Proto-Indo-European *sh₂un-, *sóh₂wl̥.

examples: Wie heeft er twee kilo zout in mijn soep gedaan? Nu smaakt het ranzig!
Hou jij van zoute haring?
Dit is echt veel te zout!

zout.e: From Middle Dutch sout, from Old Dutch *salt, from Proto-West Germanic *salt. The noun is from Proto-Germanic *saltą, the adjective from *saltaz. Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *séh₂l-, *séh₂ls. Compare German Salz, West Frisian sâlt, English salt, Danish salt.

s.def: hey, used to draw the addressed person's attention.
examples: Zeg, Leen, kom eens hier. – Hey, Leen, come here for a moment.

s.def: Used for emphasis, especially if the message is sudden or contrastive.
examples: Nee, ik kom niet, hoepel op zeg.
Wat een vervelende gozer zeg.
Dat was geen slimme zet.
Hij is aan zet.

zuur.e:
From Middle Dutch suur, from Old Dutch *sūr, from Proto-West Germanic *sūr, from Proto-Germanic *sūraz.
Cognate with Low German suur, West Frisian soer, German sauer, English sour, Danish sur.

s.def: an exam term at university or an institution of intermediate tertiary education

zwaard.e: From Middle Dutch swert, swaert, from Old Dutch swert, from Proto-West Germanic *swerd, from Proto-Germanic *swerdą, of uncertain origin; see there for more.

zo.e: From Middle Dutch sô, from Old Dutch sō, from Proto-West Germanic *swā, from a merger of Proto-Germanic *swa and *swē.

zestig.e: From Middle Dutch sestich, also tsestich with t- from tsēventich, from Old Dutch *sestig, from Proto-Germanic *sehs tigiwiz.

* From Middle Dutch swemmen, from Old Dutch *swimman, from Proto-West Germanic *swimman, from Proto-Germanic *swimmaną.

From Middle Dutch saterdach, from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic *Sāturnas dag, calque of Latin dies Saturni. Compare Low German Saterdag, West Frisian saterdei, English Saturday.

zilver.e: From Middle Dutch silver, from Old Dutch silver, from Proto-Germanic *silubrą.

examples: Als je niets beters te zeggen hebt, zeg dan niets.

zeggen.e: From Middle Dutch seggen, from Old Dutch seggen, from Proto-West Germanic *saggjan. The irregular past tense is a common Ingvaeonic development of the northern dialects, seen also in brein and zeil. (In an unrelated, later development the same also happened in Middle High German sagen.)

examples: De boot is gezonken.

zinken.e: From Middle Dutch sinken, from Old Dutch *sincan, from Proto-West Germanic *sinkwan, from Proto-Indo-European *sengʷ- (“to fall, sink”).

zacht.e: From Middle Dutch sachte, from Old Dutch sāfti, from Proto-West Germanic *samftī.

zaak.e:
From Middle Dutch sāke, from Old Dutch *saka, from Proto-West Germanic *saku, from Proto-Germanic *sakō.
Cognate with English sake, West Frisian saak (“cause; business”), Low German Saak, German Sache (“thing; matter; cause; legal cause”), Yiddish זאַך (zakh), Danish sag, Swedish and Norwegian sa...

zorg.e:
From Middle Dutch sorge, sorghe, surghe, from Old Dutch sorga, from Proto-West Germanic *sorgu, from Proto-Germanic *surgō, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *swergʰ- (“watch over, worry; be ill, suffer”).

zonder.e: From Middle Dutch sonder, from Old Dutch sundar, from Proto-Germanic *sundraz. Cognate with English sunder.

zwak.e: From Middle Dutch swac, from Old Dutch *swak, from Proto-West Germanic *swak.

zeker.e: From Middle Dutch sēker, from Old Dutch *sikur, from Proto-West Germanic *sikur (“free, secure”), borrowed from Latin sēcūrus.

zelfde.e: From Middle Dutch selfde. This was formed from the older selve (modern zelve), probably with -de from some of the ordinal numbers. Further etymology is as for zelf.

zorgen.e: From Middle Dutch sorgen, from Old Dutch sorgon, from Proto-West Germanic *sorgēn, from Proto-Germanic *surgāną. By surface analysis, zorg + -en.

zouten.e: From Middle Dutch souten, from Old Dutch *saltan, from Proto-Germanic *saltaną. The verb was originally a class 7 strong verb, but the verb became weak early on and only the strong past participle was preserved. The past form would have been *zielt, parallel to houden and hield.

examples: Mijn pen is zoek.

zesde.e: From Middle Dutch seste, from Old Dutch *sesto, from Proto-Germanic *sehstô.

zevende.e: From Middle Dutch sēvende, from Old Dutch *sivondo, from Proto-Germanic *sebundô.

zenegroen.e:
From Middle Dutch singroene, sindegroen, from Old Dutch *singrōni, from Proto-West Germanic *singrōnijā. Possibly an early calque of Latin sempervīvum, itself a calque of Ancient Greek ἀείζωον (aeízōon).
This word represents the sole inherited survival of the Germanic prefix *sin- in modern Dutch...
